description: Lineage endpoints (GET /lineages/{lineage_id} and GET /lineages/{lineage_id}/current) MUST apply the same per-context visibility rules as GET /contexts/{ctx_id}. Knowing a lineage_id MUST NOT grant access to bodies that ctx_id-level access control would deny. RFC-ACDP-0004 §5.4.

lineages.note: Two-version restricted lineage. Both versions share the same audience.
Two-version lineage whose current head is private to the producer.

scenarios.name: Full lineage — stranger sees zero versions of a restricted lineage (empty array, not 404)
scenarios.notes: Every version is restricted and the requester is in no audience. Each version would return not_found via GET /contexts/{ctx_id}, so each is omitted. The lineage itself exists, so the endpoint returns an empty array (HTTP 200), NOT not_found.

scenarios.name: Full lineage — audience member sees all restricted versions
scenarios.notes: The requester is in the audience of every version, so the full history is returned, ordered by version ascending.

scenarios.name: Full lineage — partial visibility leaves a version gap (not an error)
scenarios.notes: v1 is public (visible); v2 is private (omitted). The result is the visible subsequence — version 1 only. The gap at version 2 is expected; a consumer MUST NOT treat a missing version number as an error.

scenarios.name: Current — stranger denied for a private current head (not_found, indistinguishable from missing lineage)
scenarios.notes: The current head (v2) is private. The registry MUST NOT fall back to the superseded public v1 — current is the newest non-superseded version, and v1 is superseded. With no authorized non-superseded version, the endpoint returns not_found, indistinguishable from a lineage that does not exist.

scenarios.name: Current — producer sees the private current head
scenarios.notes: The producer is always in the effective audience for their own contexts and retrieves the private current head.
